top.value: IKI Secretariat, Zukunft - Umwelt - Gesellschaft (ZUG) gGmbH, Stresemannstraße 69 - 71, 10963 Berlin, Germany

The Mitigation Action Facility is an international, multi-donor climate mitigation programme that supports countries worldwide in achieving their climate goals. It promotes projects that effectively and sustainably reduce greenhouse gas emissions in the energy, industry, and transport sectors.  Since 2012, it has supported over 60 projects in 38 countries with EUR 835 million. In annual competitive calls, Applicants can apply for project funding. Highly effective projects receive grants to advance climate-friendly technologies and practices.  Decisions on strategy and project selection are made by the donor group, currently comprising Germany, the United Kingdom, Denmark, the EU, and CIFF. The GIZ provides the Technical Support Unit (TSU), which functions as the secretariat and portfolio manager. The TSU oversees project calls, selection processes, and ongoing project support, evaluation, and further development.

The project was part of a seven-project programme implemented by WHO that aimed at strengthening the capacity of participating countries to identify the health-related risks of climate change. To this end the project partners conducted a National Climate Change Vulnerability Assessment for the health sector and developed a national adaptation strategy based on its findings. The project also aimed at expanding local know-how in this field by training experts and making information more widely available in the relevant language.

The challenges of the interdependence between energy, water and land use, are exacerbated by climate change in Central Asia. The project enhances economic and financial analytical work at regional and national level and nexus-relevant financing opportunities will be explored and identified. It will demonstrate the “business case” for co-operation on the nexus, enhance capacity needed for operationalising the nexus approach, and create political momentum for action. The project facilitates the political and technical dialogue for implementing the nexus approach and enhances regional co-operation in various regional and national conferences and capacity development activities. Such tools and knowledge products will also facilitate further engagement with the private sector in nexus-related investments by providing enhanced evidence on the benefits and trade-offs that are inherent to nexus projects.

The project supports the current push for ambitious and implementable NDCs by providing evidence of the economic and social opportunities of an ambitious and just transition. The project provides insights on how to realise these opportunities by underlying governance structures needed to support the delivery of ambitious new national commitments, and good practice policies to unlock the public and private finance and investment needed.

The significant proliferation of environmental crimes is causing serious negative impact on climate change and biodiversity loss at a global scale. Their devastating consequences can be observed in five major areas: fisheries, forestry, mining, pollution and wildlife. The objective of this project is to enhance law enforcement efforts and international cooperation to detect and disrupt environmental crimes, while also exploring pathways to facilitate the engagement from civil society that may collaborate with law enforcement authorities via the detection and reporting of suspected illicit activities that could pose a threat to environmental security. This will be achieved through a series of initiatives that will aim at strengthening the law enforcement capabilities and operational actions of project beneficiary countries, while increasing the availability and exchange of criminal data and intelligence to enhance transnational investigations of environmental crimes.
Support of the Implementation of the Paris Agreement in countries and international negotiations

The impacts of climate change threaten the livelihoods of Mexican small farmers. Therefore the project promotes adaptation, restoration and conservation of rural landscapes to increase their resilience and to ensure food security and local income. It helps small farmers in the states of Campeche, Tabasco, Chiapas, Jalisco, Michoacan, Oaxaca, Puebla, Hidalgo and Tlaxcala to adapt and to increase their production. Ecosystem-based adaptation strategies include sustainable production and integrated land use planning approaches that equally take into account food, forest, biodiversity and climate change. Innovative finance mechanisms support this process. The project also promotes land use management policies at national and sub-national level to help Mexico to achieve its goals in the areas of SDGs, NDCs and biodiversity.

According to the World Economic Forum, USD44 trillion of global economic output, around half the global economy, is at moderate or significant exposure to nature-related risks. The Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) is developing a market-led, integrated global risk management and disclosure framework. This framework forms the basis for companies to report on nature impacts and dependencies, and risks and opportunities. Such disclosures provide the basis for shifting the flow of global capital to transition the global economy to nature-positive outcomes. This project will help business and financial institutions around the world report nature-related financial disclosures in their financial statements. Such information will better inform governments, financial markets, community groups and citizens as they make decisions for more sustainable prosperity.

According to projections, the global consumption of climate-damaging F gases for refrigeration and air conditioning will have quadrupled by 2030. The project supported the comprehensive international control of F gases. To this end, it bundled, harmonised and strengthened relevant international initiatives. The project’s strategic approach involved supporting six key partner countries in formulating their individual climate change mitigation contributions, introducing transformation policies, supporting the further development and implementation of NDCs and removing barriers to the use of natural refrigerants & propellants. Thanks to institutionalised structures and networks, the project achieved a multiplier effect in the six UN regions.

Accelerating the transition to decarbonized power systems and industrial sectors is critical to achieving global climate neutrality. This is especially urgent in emerging and developing economies, where emissions are expected to grow significantly alongside economic development. Local think tanks and civil society actors are key to this shift but often lack skills and influence. Strengthening their role helps shape smarter policies and drive a fair, low-carbon transformation in power and industry. This project supports that by training professionals – with a special focus on women – from think tanks and civil society organisations in Latin America, Southeast Asia and beyond. They gain skills to develop practical, evidence-based solutions, shape public debate and support climate-friendly policies. Through regional partnerships, co-development and a global network, the project empowers local voices to drive change for a just and sustainable transformation.
